The **Tokugawa shogunate**,[[a]](#cite_note-20) also known as the **Edo shogunate**,[[b]](#cite_note-21) was the [military government](/wiki/Military_government "Military government") of [Japan](/wiki/Japan "Japan") during the [Edo period](/wiki/Edo_period "Edo period") from 1603 to 1868.[[20]](#cite_note-nussbaum978-22)[[21]](#cite_note-Edo-jidai-23)[[22]](#cite_note-24)

The Tokugawa [shogunate](/wiki/Shogun "Shogun") was established by [Tokugawa Ieyasu](/wiki/Tokugawa_Ieyasu "Tokugawa Ieyasu") after victory at the [Battle of Sekigahara](/wiki/Battle_of_Sekigahara "Battle of Sekigahara"), ending the civil wars of the [Sengoku period](/wiki/Sengoku_period "Sengoku period") following the collapse of the [Ashikaga shogunate](/wiki/Ashikaga_shogunate "Ashikaga shogunate"). Ieyasu became the *[shōgun](/wiki/Sh%C5%8Dgun "Shōgun"),* and the [Tokugawa clan](/wiki/Tokugawa_clan "Tokugawa clan") governed Japan from [Edo Castle](/wiki/Edo_Castle "Edo Castle") in the eastern city of [Edo](/wiki/Edo_(Tokyo) "Edo (Tokyo)") ([Tokyo](/wiki/Tokyo "Tokyo")) along with the *[daimyō](/wiki/Daimy%C5%8D "Daimyō")* lords of the [samurai](/wiki/Samurai "Samurai") class.[[23]](#cite_note-25)[[24]](#cite_note-nussbaum976-26)[[21]](#cite_note-Edo-jidai-23)

The Tokugawa shogunate organized Japanese society under the strict [Tokugawa class system](/wiki/Edo_society "Edo society") and banned the entry of most foreigners under the isolationist policies of *[Sakoku](/wiki/Sakoku "Sakoku")* to promote political stability. Japanese subjects were also barred from leaving the country. The Tokugawa shoguns governed Japan in a feudal system, with each *daimyō* administering a *[han](/wiki/Han_system "Han system")* (feudal domain), although the country was still nominally organized as [imperial provinces](/wiki/Provinces_of_Japan "Provinces of Japan"). Under the Tokugawa shogunate, Japan experienced rapid economic growth and urbanization, which led to the rise of the merchant class and *[Ukiyo](/wiki/Ukiyo "Ukiyo")* culture.

The Tokugawa shogunate declined during the *[Bakumatsu](/wiki/Bakumatsu "Bakumatsu")* period from 1853 and was overthrown by supporters of the [Imperial Court](/wiki/Imperial_Court_in_Kyoto "Imperial Court in Kyoto") in the [Meiji Restoration](/wiki/Meiji_Restoration "Meiji Restoration") in 1868. The [Empire of Japan](/wiki/Empire_of_Japan "Empire of Japan") was established under the [Meiji government](/wiki/Government_of_Meiji_Japan "Government of Meiji Japan"), and Tokugawa loyalists continued to fight in the [Boshin War](/wiki/Boshin_War "Boshin War") until the defeat of the [Republic of Ezo](/wiki/Republic_of_Ezo "Republic of Ezo") at the [Battle of Hakodate](/wiki/Battle_of_Hakodate "Battle of Hakodate") in June 1869.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tokugawa_shogunate&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

See also: [Sengoku period](/wiki/Sengoku_period "Sengoku period"), [Tokugawa Ieyasu](/wiki/Tokugawa_Ieyasu "Tokugawa Ieyasu"), and [Bakumatsu](/wiki/Bakumatsu "Bakumatsu")

Further information: [Edo period](/wiki/Edo_period "Edo period")

Following the Sengoku period ("Warring States period"), the central government had been largely re-established by [Oda Nobunaga](/wiki/Oda_Nobunaga "Oda Nobunaga") during the [Azuchi–Momoyama period](/wiki/Azuchi%E2%80%93Momoyama_period "Azuchi–Momoyama period"). After the Battle of Sekigahara in 1600, central authority fell to Tokugawa Ieyasu.[[20]](#cite_note-nussbaum978-22) While many *daimyō* who fought against him were extinguished or had their holdings reduced, Ieyasu was committed to retaining the *daimyō* and the *han* (domains) as components under his new shogunate.[[25]](#cite_note-brown_1997-27) *daimyō* who sided with Ieyasu were rewarded, and some of Ieyasu's former vassals were made *daimyō* and were located strategically throughout the country.[[25]](#cite_note-brown_1997-27) The [*sankin-kotai*](/wiki/Sankin-k%C5%8Dtai "Sankin-kōtai") policy, in an effort to constrain rebellions by the *daimyō*, mandated the housing of wives and children of the *daimyō* in the capital as hostages.[[26]](#cite_note-paik_steele_tanaka_2017-28)

A period of peace occurred between the [Siege of Osaka](/wiki/Siege_of_Osaka "Siege of Osaka") in 1615 and the [Keian Uprising](/wiki/Keian_Uprising "Keian Uprising") in 1651. This period saw the [bakufu](/wiki/Bakufu "Bakufu") (shogunate's administration) prioritise civil administration, while civil society witnessed a surge in trade and industrial activities. Trade under the reign of Ieyasu saw much new wealth created by mining and goods manufacturing, which resulted in a rural population flow to urban areas.[[27]](#cite_note-sansom_1958-29) By the [Genroku period](/wiki/Genroku_period "Genroku period") (1688–1704) Japan saw a period of material prosperity and the blossoming of the arts, such as the early development of *[ukiyo-e](/wiki/Ukiyo-e "Ukiyo-e")* by [Moronobu](/wiki/Hishikawa_Moronobu "Hishikawa Moronobu"). The reign of [Tokugawa Yoshimune](/wiki/Tokugawa_Yoshimune "Tokugawa Yoshimune") (1716–1745) saw poor harvests and a fall in tax revenue in the early 1720s; as a result he pushed for the [Kyoho reforms](/wiki/Kyoho_Reforms "Kyoho Reforms") to repair the finances of the bakufu as he believed the military aristocracy was losing its power against the rich merchants and landowners.[[27]](#cite_note-sansom_1958-29)

[Society in the Tokugawa period](/wiki/Edo_society "Edo society"), unlike in previous shogunates, was supposedly based on the strict class hierarchy originally established by [Toyotomi Hideyoshi](/wiki/Toyotomi_Hideyoshi "Toyotomi Hideyoshi"). The *daimyō* (lords) were at the top, followed by the warrior-caste of samurai, with the farmers, artisans, and traders ranking below. In some parts of the country, particularly smaller regions, *daimyō,* and samurai were more or less identical, since *daimyō* might be trained as samurai, and samurai might act as local rulers.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The largely inflexible nature of this social stratification system unleashed disruptive forces over time. Taxes on the peasantry were set at fixed amounts that did not account for inflation or other changes in monetary value. As a result, the tax revenues collected by the samurai landowners increasingly declined over time. A 2017 study found that peasant rebellions and desertion lowered tax rates and inhibited state growth in the Tokugawa shogunate.[[26]](#cite_note-paik_steele_tanaka_2017-28) By the mid-18th century, both the *shōgun* and *daimyō* were hampered by financial difficulties, whereas more wealth flowed to the merchant class. Peasant uprisings and samurai discontent became increasingly prevalent. Some reforms were enacted to attend to these issues such as the [Kansei reforms](/wiki/Emperor_K%C5%8Dkaku#Kansei_Reforms "Emperor Kōkaku") (1787–1793) by [Matsudaira Sadanobu](/wiki/Matsudaira_Sadanobu "Matsudaira Sadanobu").[[28]](#cite_note-30) He bolstered the bakufu's rice stockpiles and mandated *daimyō* to follow suit. He cut down urban spending, allocated reserves for potential famines, and urged city-dwelling peasants to return to rural areas.[[29]](#cite_note-mikiso_perez_2015-31)

By 1800, Japan included five cities with over 100,000 residents, and three among the world's twenty cities that had more than 300,000 inhabitants. Edo likely claimed the title of the world's most populous city, housing over one million people.[[30]](#cite_note-32)

### Late Tokugawa shogunate (1853–1867)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tokugawa_shogunate&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Late Tokugawa shogunate (1853–1867)")]

Main article: [Bakumatsu](/wiki/Bakumatsu "Bakumatsu")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5c/Satsuma-samurai-during-boshin-war-period.jpg/250px-Satsuma-samurai-during-boshin-war-period.jpg)](/wiki/File:Satsuma-samurai-during-boshin-war-period.jpg)

[Samurai](/wiki/Samurai "Samurai") of the [Shimazu clan](/wiki/Shimazu_clan "Shimazu clan")

The late Tokugawa shogunate (*[Bakumatsu](/wiki/Bakumatsu "Bakumatsu")*) was the period between 1853 and 1867, during which Japan ended its [isolationist foreign policy](/wiki/Isolationist_foreign_policy "Isolationist foreign policy") called *[sakoku](/wiki/Sakoku "Sakoku")* and modernized from a [feudal](/wiki/Feudal "Feudal") shogunate to the [Meiji government](/wiki/Meiji_period "Meiji period"). The 1850s saw growing resentment by the *[tozama daimyōs](/wiki/Tozama_daimy%C5%8D "Tozama daimyō")* and [anti-Western sentiment](/wiki/Anti-Western_sentiment "Anti-Western sentiment") following the arrival of a [U.S. Navy](/wiki/United_States_Navy "United States Navy") fleet under the command of [Matthew C. Perry](/wiki/Matthew_C._Perry "Matthew C. Perry") (which led to the forced opening of Japan). The major ideological and political factions during this period were divided into the pro-imperialist *[Ishin Shishi](/wiki/Ishin_Shishi "Ishin Shishi")* ([nationalist](/wiki/Nationalist "Nationalist") [patriots](/wiki/Patriotism "Patriotism")) and the shogunate forces; aside from the dominant two groups, other factions attempted to use the chaos of the Bakumatsu era to seize personal power.[[31]](#cite_note-SHINSENGUMI-33)

An alliance of *daimyō* and the emperor succeeded in overthrowing the shogunate, which came to an official end in 1868 with the resignation of the 15th Tokugawa shogun*,* [Tokugawa Yoshinobu](/wiki/Tokugawa_Yoshinobu "Tokugawa Yoshinobu"), leading to the "restoration" ([王政復古](https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E7%8E%8B%E6%94%BF%E5%BE%A9%E5%8F%A4_(%E6%97%A5%E6%9C%AC) "ja:王政復古 (日本)"), *Ōsei fukko*) of imperial rule. Some loyal retainers of the shogun continued to fight during the [Boshin War](/wiki/Boshin_War "Boshin War") that followed but were eventually defeated in the notable [Battle of Toba–Fushimi](/wiki/Battle_of_Toba%E2%80%93Fushimi "Battle of Toba–Fushimi").[[32]](#cite_note-LS-34)

## Religious policy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tokugawa_shogunate&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Religious policy")]

### Christians under the Shogunate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tokugawa_shogunate&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Christians under the Shogunate")]

See also: [Christianity in Japan](/wiki/Christianity_in_Japan "Christianity in Japan")

Followers of [Catholic](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church") Christians first began appearing in Japan during the 16th century.[[33]](#cite_note-35)

In 1600, when English sailor [William Adams](/wiki/William_Adams_(samurai) "William Adams (samurai)") and his Dutch colleague [Jan Joosten](/wiki/Jan_Joosten "Jan Joosten") arrived at Japan, they told Ieyasu about the world situation, including that there were many conflicts in Europe, and that the Jesuits and other Catholics (e.g. Portuguese, Spanish), who had been proselytizing Christianity in Japan, and the Protestants (e.g. Dutch, English) were on different sides and were in conflict with each other. Ieyasu reportedly took a liking to them for their frankness and regarded them as trustworthy.[[34]](#cite_note-Japaaan191464-36)[[35]](#cite_note-rekishijin24815-37)

While at first tolerant of [Christianity](/wiki/Christianity_in_Japan "Christianity in Japan"),[[36]](#cite_note-38) [Tokugawa Ieyasu](/wiki/Tokugawa_Ieyasu "Tokugawa Ieyasu") soon began to see it as a growing threat to the stability of the shogunate.[[37]](#cite_note-39) His attitude changed after 1613, and persecution of [Christians](/wiki/Kirishitan "Kirishitan") sharply increased, with Ieyasu completely banning [Catholicism](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church") in 1614.[[38]](#cite_note-40)[[39]](#cite_note-TotmanTokugawa-41)

The hostility of Ieyasu towards Catholics was shown when he replaced Jesuit translator [João Rodrigues Tçuzu](/wiki/Jo%C3%A3o_Rodrigues_T%C3%A7uzu "João Rodrigues Tçuzu") with [William Adams](/wiki/William_Adams_(samurai) "William Adams (samurai)") in his court in the aftermath of the [Nossa Senhora da Graça incident](/wiki/Nossa_Senhora_da_Gra%C3%A7a_incident "Nossa Senhora da Graça incident") in Nagasaki.[[40]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMilton2011[httpsbooksgooglecombooksidjZqNl1GnkoYCpgPT38-42)[[41]](#cite_note-Takase_Koichiro_2002_383–384-43) This change of attitude is believed to be due to the [Okamoto Daihachi incident](/wiki/Okamoto_Daihachi_incident "Okamoto Daihachi incident"), where a Catholic *daimyō* and shogun's official were accused of a series of crimes.[[42]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMurdochYamagata1903498-44)

The [Shimabara Rebellion](/wiki/Shimabara_Rebellion "Shimabara Rebellion") is often portrayed as a Christian rebellion against violent suppression by Matsukura Katsuie. However the main academic understanding is that the rebellion was mainly by peasants against Matsukura's misgovernance, with Christians later joining the rebellion.[[43]](#cite_note-45) The system which introduced by the Shogunate to stamp out Catholicism after Shimabara rebellion was the [Danka system](/wiki/Danka_system "Danka system"), which makes affiliation of every Household in Japan to the Buddhist temple being compulsory.[[44]](#cite_note-46)

Ieyasu's ban of Christianity is often linked with the creation of the *[sakoku](/wiki/Sakoku "Sakoku")* seclusionist policies in the 1630s. His successor shoguns followed his policy, compounding upon existing laws by Ieyasu.[[39]](#cite_note-TotmanTokugawa-41) The ban on Christianity was enforced via decrees of expulsion and mass-executions in 1613, 1622 ([Great Genna Martyrdom](/wiki/Great_Genna_Martyrdom "Great Genna Martyrdom")), 1623 ([Great Martyrdom of Edo](/wiki/Great_Martyrdom_of_Edo "Great Martyrdom of Edo")) 1630, 1632 and 1634.[[45]](#cite_note-47)

## Government

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tokugawa_shogunate&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Government")]

### Shogunate and domains

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tokugawa_shogunate&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Shogunate and domains")]

The *bakuhan* system (*bakuhan taisei* 幕藩体制) was the [feudal](/wiki/Feudalism "Feudalism") political system in the Edo period of Japan.[[7]](#cite_note-britannica-7) *Baku* is an abbreviation of *bakufu*, meaning "[military government](/wiki/Military_government "Military government")"—that is, the shogunate. The *han* were the domains headed by *daimyō*.[[7]](#cite_note-britannica-7) Beginning from Ieyasu's appointment as shogun in 1603, but especially after the Tokugawa [victory in Osaka](/wiki/Siege_of_Osaka "Siege of Osaka") in 1615, various policies were implemented to assert the shogunate's control, which severely curtailed the *daimyō'* independence.[[25]](#cite_note-brown_1997-27) The number of *daimyō* varied but stabilized at around 270.[[25]](#cite_note-brown_1997-27)

The *bakuhan* system split feudal power between the shogunate in Edo and the *daimyō* with domains throughout Japan.[[46]](#cite_note-perez_2009-48) The *shōgun* and lords were all *daimyō*: feudal lords with their own bureaucracies, policies, and territories.[[46]](#cite_note-perez_2009-48) Provinces had a degree of sovereignty and were allowed an independent administration of the *han* in exchange for loyalty to the *shōgun*, who was responsible for foreign relations, national security,[[46]](#cite_note-perez_2009-48) coinage, weights, measures, and transportation.[[25]](#cite_note-brown_1997-27)

The *shōgun* also administered the most powerful *han*, the hereditary fief of the House of Tokugawa, which also included many gold and silver mines.[[46]](#cite_note-perez_2009-48) Towards the end of the shogunate, the Tokugawa clan held around 7 million *[koku](/wiki/Koku "Koku")* of land (天領 tenryō), including 2.6–2.7 million *koku* held by direct vassals, out of 30 million in the country.[[29]](#cite_note-mikiso_perez_2015-31) The other 23 million *koku* were held by other *daimyō*.[[29]](#cite_note-mikiso_perez_2015-31)

The number of *han* (roughly 270) fluctuated throughout the Edo period.[[47]](#cite_note-gregorysmits-49) They were ranked by size, which was measured as the number of *koku* of rice that the domain produced each year.[[29]](#cite_note-mikiso_perez_2015-31) One *koku* was the amount of rice necessary to feed one adult male for one year. The minimum number for a *daimyō* was ten thousand *koku*;[[47]](#cite_note-gregorysmits-49) the largest, apart from the *shōgun*, was more than a million *koku*.[[29]](#cite_note-mikiso_perez_2015-31)

#### Policies to control the *daimyō*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tokugawa_shogunate&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Policies to control the daimyō")]

The main policies of the shogunate on the *daimyō* included:

* The principle was that each *daimyō* (including those who were previously independent of the Tokugawa family) submitted to the shogunate, and each *han* required the shogunate's recognition and was subject to its land redistributions.[[25]](#cite_note-brown_1997-27): 192–93 *daimyō* swore allegiance to each shogun and acknowledged the Laws for Warrior Houses or *buke shohatto*.[[29]](#cite_note-mikiso_perez_2015-31)
* The *[sankin-kōtai](/wiki/Sankin-k%C5%8Dtai "Sankin-kōtai")* ("alternate attendance") system, required *daimyō* to travel to and reside in Edo every other year, and for their families to remain in Edo during their absence.
* The *[ikkoku ichijyō rei](/w/index.php?title=Ikkoku_ichijy%C5%8D_rei&action=edit&redlink=1 "Ikkoku ichijyō rei (page does not exist)") [[ja](https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E4%B8%80%E5%9B%BD%E4%B8%80%E5%9F%8E%E4%BB%A4 "ja:一国一城令")]* (一国一城令), allowed each *daimyō*'s *han* to retain only one fortification, at the *daimyō*'s residence.[[25]](#cite_note-brown_1997-27): 194
* The [Laws for the Military Houses](/wiki/Buke_shohatto "Buke shohatto") (*buke shohatto*), the first of which in 1615 forbade the building of new fortifications or repairing existing ones without *bakufu* approval, admitting fugitives of the shogunate, and arranging marriages of the *daimyō'* families without official permission.[[25]](#cite_note-brown_1997-27) Additional rules on the samurai were issued over the years.[[25]](#cite_note-brown_1997-27)[[29]](#cite_note-mikiso_perez_2015-31)

Although the shogun issued certain laws, such as the *buke shohatto* on the *daimyō* and the rest of the samurai class, each *han* administered its autonomous system of laws and [taxation](/wiki/Taxation "Taxation").[[46]](#cite_note-perez_2009-48) The *shōgun* did not interfere in a *han*'s governance unless major incompetence (such as large rebellions) was shown, nor were central taxes issued.[[46]](#cite_note-perez_2009-48) Instead, each *han* provided feudal duties, such as maintaining roads and official courier stations, building canals and harbors, providing troops, and relieving famines.[[46]](#cite_note-perez_2009-48) *Daimyōs* were strategically placed to check each other, and the *sankin-kōtai* system ensured that *daimyō* or their family were always in Edo, observed by the shogun.[[46]](#cite_note-perez_2009-48)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/94/Edo_P2.jpg/250px-Edo_P2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Edo_P2.jpg)

[Edo Castle](/wiki/Edo_Castle "Edo Castle"), 17th century

The shogunate had the power to discard, annex, and transform domains, although they were rarely and carefully exercised after the early years of the shogunate, to prevent *daimyō* from banding together.[[46]](#cite_note-perez_2009-48) The *sankin-kōtai* system of alternative residence required each *daimyō* to reside in alternate years between the *han* and the court in Edo.[[46]](#cite_note-perez_2009-48) During their absences from Edo, it was also required that they leave their family as hostages until their return. The hostages and the huge expenditure *sankin-kōtai* imposed on each *han* helped to ensure loyalty to the *shōgun*.[[46]](#cite_note-perez_2009-48) By the 1690s, the vast majority of *daimyō* would be born in Edo, and most would consider it their homes.[[25]](#cite_note-brown_1997-27) Some *daimyō* had little interest in their domains and needed to be begged to return "home".[[25]](#cite_note-brown_1997-27)

In return for the centralization, peace among the *daimyō* was maintained; unlike in the [Sengoku period](/wiki/Sengoku_period "Sengoku period"), *daimyō* no longer worried about conflicts with one another.[[25]](#cite_note-brown_1997-27) In addition, hereditary succession was guaranteed as internal usurpations within domains were not recognized by the shogunate.[[25]](#cite_note-brown_1997-27)

#### Classification of *daimyō*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tokugawa_shogunate&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Classification of daimyō")]

The Tokugawa clan further ensured loyalty by maintaining a dogmatic insistence on loyalty to the *shōgun*. Daimyos were classified into three main categories:[[29]](#cite_note-mikiso_perez_2015-31)

* *[Shinpan](/wiki/Shinpan "Shinpan")* ("relatives") were six clans established by sons of Ieyasu, as well as certain sons of the 8th and 9th shoguns, who were made *daimyō*.[[29]](#cite_note-mikiso_perez_2015-31) They would provide an heir to the shogunate if the shogun did not have an heir.[[29]](#cite_note-mikiso_perez_2015-31)
* *[Fudai](/wiki/Fudai_daimy%C5%8D "Fudai daimyō")* ("hereditary") were mostly vassals of Ieyasu and the Tokugawa clan before the [Battle of Sekigahara](/wiki/Battle_of_Sekigahara "Battle of Sekigahara").[[29]](#cite_note-mikiso_perez_2015-31) They ruled their *han* (estate) and served as high officials in the shogunate, although their *han* tended to be smaller compared to the *tozama* domains.[[29]](#cite_note-mikiso_perez_2015-31)
* *[Tozama](/wiki/Tozama_daimy%C5%8D "Tozama daimyō")* ("outsiders") were around 100 *daimyō*, most of whom became vassals of the Tokugawa clan after the Battle of Sekigahara. Some fought against Tokugawa forces, although some were neutral or even fought on the side of the Tokugawa clan, as allies rather than vassals.[[29]](#cite_note-mikiso_perez_2015-31) The *tozama daimyō* tend to have the largest *han*, with 11 of the 16 largest *daimyō* in this category.[[29]](#cite_note-mikiso_perez_2015-31)

The *tozama daimyō* who fought against the Tokugawa clan in the Battle of Sekigahara had their estates reduced substantially.[[29]](#cite_note-mikiso_perez_2015-31) They were often placed in mountainous or far away areas, or placed between most trusted *daimyō*.[[29]](#cite_note-mikiso_perez_2015-31) Early in the Edo period, the shogunate viewed the *tozama* as the least likely to be loyal; over time, strategic marriages and the entrenchment of the system made the *tozama* less likely to rebel. In the end, however, it was still the great *tozama* of [Satsuma](/wiki/Satsuma_Domain "Satsuma Domain"), [Chōshū](/wiki/Ch%C5%8Dsh%C5%AB_Domain "Chōshū Domain") and [Tosa](/wiki/Tosa_Domain "Tosa Domain"), and to a lesser extent [Saga](/wiki/Saga_Domain "Saga Domain"), that brought down the shogunate. These four states are called the Four Western Clans, or [Satchotohi](/w/index.php?title=Satchotohi&action=edit&redlink=1 "Satchotohi (page does not exist)") for short.[[48]](#cite_note-Nussbaum;_Satchotohi-50)

### Relations with the Emperor

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tokugawa_shogunate&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Relations with the Emperor")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ee/Tokugawa_Ieyasu_-_Directories_of_Famous_Generals_of_Japan.jpg/250px-Tokugawa_Ieyasu_-_Directories_of_Famous_Generals_of_Japan.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tokugawa_Ieyasu_-_Directories_of_Famous_Generals_of_Japan.jpg)

An *[ukiyo-e](/wiki/Ukiyo-e "Ukiyo-e")* by [Yoshitoshi](/wiki/Tsukioka_Yoshitoshi "Tsukioka Yoshitoshi") depicting the scene when Ieyasu had an audience with [Emperor Go-Yōzei](/wiki/Emperor_Go-Y%C5%8Dzei "Emperor Go-Yōzei")

Regardless of the political title of the Emperor, the *shōguns* of the Tokugawa family controlled Japan.[[49]](#cite_note-51) The shogunate secured a nominal grant of administration (体制, *taisei*) by the [Imperial Court in Kyoto](/wiki/Imperial_Court_in_Kyoto "Imperial Court in Kyoto") to the Tokugawa family.[[47]](#cite_note-gregorysmits-49) While the Emperor officially had the prerogative of appointing the *shōgun* and received generous subsidies, he had virtually no say in state affairs.[[46]](#cite_note-perez_2009-48) The shogunate issued the [Laws for the Imperial and Court Officials](/wiki/Kinchu_narabini_kuge_shohatto "Kinchu narabini kuge shohatto") to set out its relationship with the [Imperial family](/wiki/Imperial_House_of_Japan "Imperial House of Japan") and the *[kuge](/wiki/Kuge "Kuge")* (imperial court officials), and specified that the Emperor should dedicate himself to scholarship and poetry.[[50]](#cite_note-lillehoj_2011-52) The shogunate also appointed a liaison, the *[Kyoto Shoshidai](/wiki/Kyoto_Shoshidai "Kyoto Shoshidai")* (*Shogun's Representative in Kyoto*), to deal with the Emperor, court and nobility.

Towards the end of the shogunate, however, after centuries of the Emperor having very little say in state affairs and being secluded in his [Kyoto palace](/wiki/Kyoto_Imperial_Palace "Kyoto Imperial Palace"), and in the wake of the reigning *shōgun*, [Tokugawa Iemochi](/wiki/Tokugawa_Iemochi "Tokugawa Iemochi"), marrying the sister of [Emperor Kōmei](/wiki/Emperor_K%C5%8Dmei "Emperor Kōmei") (r. 1846–1867), in 1862, the Imperial Court in Kyoto began to enjoy increased political influence.[[51]](#cite_note-53) The Emperor would occasionally be consulted on various policies and the shogun even made a trip to Kyoto to visit the Emperor.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Government administration would be formally returned from the *shōgun* to the Emperor during the [Meiji Restoration](/wiki/Meiji_Restoration "Meiji Restoration") in 1868.

Further information: [Red Seal ships](/wiki/Red_Seal_ships "Red Seal ships"), [Nanban trade](/wiki/Nanban_trade "Nanban trade"), and [Rangaku](/wiki/Rangaku "Rangaku")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/87/Grote_partij_bij_het_opperhoofd_van_Dejima.jpg/250px-Grote_partij_bij_het_opperhoofd_van_Dejima.jpg)](/wiki/File:Grote_partij_bij_het_opperhoofd_van_Dejima.jpg)

Dutch trading post in [Dejima](/wiki/Dejima "Dejima"), c. 1805

After the unification of Japan, the discovery of new silver mines and the improvement of mining techniques, the extraction and export of silver from Japan increased dramatically, particularly to Ming China. Between 1560 and 1600, the annual export of silver ranged from 33 to 49 tons, but due to the Ming ban on trade with Japan, the import of Japanese silver was facilitated by the Portuguese. In the early 17th century, Japanese silver exports continued to rise, with the import of luxury goods such as silk (reaching up to 280 tons per year in the 1630s). Silk was so abundant and inexpensive in Japan that even some peasants were able to afford it, leading to a rise in its popularity among the lower classes.[[52]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAtwell1998396–399-54)

In 1601, the Hokusan region became the domain of Tokugawa Ieyasu. Gold veins in [Sado mine](/wiki/Sado_mine "Sado mine") were discovered in the same year in the region, and the region served as an important source of revenue for the Shogunate throughout the Edo period. Its peak during the Edo period was the first half of the 17th century. Records indicate that over 400 kg of gold was extracted annually, and 10,000 kan (37.5 tons) of silver was paid to the Shogunate annually,[[53]](#cite_note-JHS-55) Making it one of the world's largest gold mines at the time.[[54]](#cite_note-56)

Foreign affairs and trade were monopolized by the shogunate, yielding an extremely large profit. Foreign trade was also permitted for the [Satsuma](/wiki/Satsuma_Domain "Satsuma Domain") and the [Tsushima domains](/wiki/Tsushima_Fuch%C5%AB_Domain "Tsushima Fuchū Domain"). [Rice](/wiki/Rice "Rice") was the main trading product of Japan during this time. [Isolationism](/wiki/Isolationism "Isolationism") was the foreign policy of Japan and trade was strictly controlled. Merchants were outsiders to the [social hierarchy](/wiki/Social_hierarchy "Social hierarchy") of Japan and were thought to be greedy.

The visits of the [Nanban](/wiki/Nanban_trade "Nanban trade") ships from Portugal were at first the main vector of trade exchanges, followed by the addition of Dutch, English, and sometimes Spanish ships.

From 1603 onward, Japan started to participate actively in foreign trade. In 1615, an embassy and trade mission under [Hasekura Tsunenaga](/wiki/Hasekura_Tsunenaga "Hasekura Tsunenaga") was sent across the Pacific to [Nueva España](/wiki/Nueva_Espa%C3%B1a "Nueva España") (New Spain) on the Japanese-built galleon [*San Juan Bautista*](/wiki/Japanese_warship_San_Juan_Bautista "Japanese warship San Juan Bautista"). Until 1635, the Shogun issued numerous permits for the so-called "[red seal ships](/wiki/Red_seal_ships "Red seal ships")" destined for the Asian trade.

After 1635 and the introduction of [seclusion laws](/wiki/Sakoku "Sakoku") (*sakoku*), inbound ships were only allowed from [China](/wiki/Qing_dynasty "Qing dynasty"), [Korea](/wiki/Joseon "Joseon"), and the [Netherlands](/wiki/Netherlands "Netherlands").

The primary source of the shogunate's income was the tax (around 40%) levied on harvests in the Tokugawa clan's personal domains (tenryō).[[29]](#cite_note-mikiso_perez_2015-31) No taxes were levied on domains of *daimyō*, who instead provided military duty, public works and [corvee](/wiki/Corv%C3%A9e "Corvée").[[29]](#cite_note-mikiso_perez_2015-31) The shogunate obtained loans from merchants, which were sometimes seen as forced donations, although commerce was often not taxed.[[29]](#cite_note-mikiso_perez_2015-31) Special levies were also imposed for infrastructure-building.[[29]](#cite_note-mikiso_perez_2015-31)

## Shogunate institution

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tokugawa_shogunate&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Shogunate institution")]

During the earliest years of the Tokugawa shogunate institution, when Tokugawa Hidetada coronated as the second shogun and Ieyasu retired, they formed dual governments, where Hidetada controlled the official court with the government central located in Edo city, Ieyasu, who now became the *Ōgosho* (retired shogun), also controlled his own informal shadow government which called "Sunpu government" with its center at [Sunpu Castle](/wiki/Sunpu_Castle "Sunpu Castle"). The membership of the Sunpu government's cabinet was consisted of trusted vassals of Ieyasu which was not included in Hidetada's cabinet. including [William Adams (samurai)](/wiki/William_Adams_(samurai) "William Adams (samurai)") and [Jan Joosten van Lodensteijn](/wiki/Jan_Joosten_van_Lodensteijn "Jan Joosten van Lodensteijn"), which Ieyasu entrusted with foreign affairs and diplomacy.[[55]](#cite_note-57)[[56]](#cite_note-58)

The earliest structure of Edo Shogunate organization has *Buke Shitsuyaku* as the highest rank. the earliest members of this office were [Ii Naomasa](/wiki/Ii_Naomasa "Ii Naomasa"), [Sakakibara Yasumasa](/wiki/Sakakibara_Yasumasa "Sakakibara Yasumasa"), and [Honda Tadakatsu](/wiki/Honda_Tadakatsu "Honda Tadakatsu").[[57]](#cite_note-59)[[58]](#cite_note-60)

The personal vassals of the Tokugawa shoguns were classified into two groups:

* the [bannermen](/wiki/Hatamoto "Hatamoto") (*hatamoto*) had the privilege to directly approach the shogun;[[29]](#cite_note-mikiso_perez_2015-31)
* the [housemen](/wiki/Gokenin "Gokenin") (*gokenin*) did not have the privilege of the shogun's audience.[[29]](#cite_note-mikiso_perez_2015-31)

By the early 18th century, out of around 22,000 personal vassals, most would have received stipends rather than domains.[[29]](#cite_note-mikiso_perez_2015-31)

### Rōjū and wakadoshiyori

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tokugawa_shogunate&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Rōjū and wakadoshiyori")]

The *[rōjū](/wiki/R%C5%8Dj%C5%AB "Rōjū")* were normally the most senior members of the shogunate.[[29]](#cite_note-mikiso_perez_2015-31) Normally, four or five men held the office, and one was on duty for a month at a time on a rotating basis.[[29]](#cite_note-mikiso_perez_2015-31) They supervised the *[ōmetsuke](/wiki/%C5%8Cmetsuke "Ōmetsuke")* (who checked on the *daimyō*), *machi*-*bugyō* (commissioners of administrative and judicial functions in major cities, especially Edo), *[ongoku bugyō](/w/index.php?title=Ongoku_bugy%C5%8D&action=edit&redlink=1 "Ongoku bugyō (page does not exist)") [[ja](https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E9%81%A0%E5%9B%BD%E5%A5%89%E8%A1%8C "ja:遠国奉行")]* (遠国奉行, the commissioners of other major cities and shogunate domains) and other officials, oversaw relations with the [Imperial Court in Kyoto](/wiki/Imperial_Court_in_Kyoto "Imperial Court in Kyoto"), [kuge](/wiki/Kuge "Kuge") (members of the nobility), daimyō, [Buddhist temples](/wiki/Buddhist_temples_in_Japan "Buddhist temples in Japan") and [Shinto shrines](/wiki/Shinto_shrine "Shinto shrine"), and attended to matters like divisions of [fiefs](/wiki/Fief "Fief"). Other *bugyō* (commissioners) in charge of finances, monasteries and shrines also reported to the rōjū.[[29]](#cite_note-mikiso_perez_2015-31) The roju conferred on especially important matters. In the administrative reforms of 1867 ([Keiō Reforms](/wiki/Kei%C5%8D_Reforms "Keiō Reforms")), the office was eliminated in favor of a bureaucratic system with ministers for the interior, finance, foreign relations, army, and navy.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3c/SakuradaGate2.jpg/250px-SakuradaGate2.jpg)](/wiki/File:SakuradaGate2.jpg)

Sakuradamon Gate of [Edo Castle](/wiki/Edo_Castle "Edo Castle") where [Ii Naosuke](/wiki/Ii_Naosuke "Ii Naosuke") was assassinated in 1860

In principle, the requirements for appointment to the office of rōjū were to be a *[fudai daimyō](/wiki/Fudai_daimy%C5%8D "Fudai daimyō")* and to have a fief assessed at 50000 *[koku](/wiki/Koku "Koku")* or more.[[29]](#cite_note-mikiso_perez_2015-31) However, there were exceptions to both criteria. Many appointees came from the offices close to the *shōgun*, such as *[soba yōnin](/w/index.php?title=Soba_y%C5%8Dnin&action=edit&redlink=1 "Soba yōnin (page does not exist)") [[ja](https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E5%81%B4%E7%94%A8%E4%BA%BA "ja:側用人")]* (側用人), [Kyoto Shoshidai](/wiki/Kyoto_Shoshidai "Kyoto Shoshidai"), and [Osaka-jō dai](/wiki/Osaka-j%C5%8D_dai "Osaka-jō dai").

Irregularly, the *shōguns* appointed a *rōjū* to the position of *[tairō](/wiki/Tair%C5%8D "Tairō")* (great elder).[[29]](#cite_note-mikiso_perez_2015-31) The office was limited to members of the [Ii](/wiki/Ii_clan "Ii clan"), [Sakai](/wiki/Sakai_clan "Sakai clan"), [Doi](/w/index.php?title=Doi_clan&action=edit&redlink=1 "Doi clan (page does not exist)"), and [Hotta clans](/wiki/Hotta_clan "Hotta clan"), but [Yanagisawa Yoshiyasu](/wiki/Yanagisawa_Yoshiyasu "Yanagisawa Yoshiyasu") was given the status of tairō as well. Among the most famous was [Ii Naosuke](/wiki/Ii_Naosuke "Ii Naosuke"), who was assassinated in 1860 outside the Sakuradamon Gate of [Edo Castle](/wiki/Edo_Castle "Edo Castle") ([Sakuradamon incident](/wiki/Sakuradamon_Incident_(1860) "Sakuradamon Incident (1860)")).

Three to five men titled the *[wakadoshiyori](/wiki/Wakadoshiyori "Wakadoshiyori")* were next in status below the *rōjū*.[[29]](#cite_note-mikiso_perez_2015-31) An outgrowth of the early six-man *[rokuninshū](/w/index.php?title=Rokuninsh%C5%AB&action=edit&redlink=1 "Rokuninshū (page does not exist)")* (六人衆, 1633–1649), the office took its name and final form in 1662. Their primary responsibility was management of the affairs of the [hatamoto](/wiki/Hatamoto "Hatamoto") and [gokenin](/wiki/Gokenin "Gokenin"), the direct vassals of the *shōgun*.[[29]](#cite_note-mikiso_perez_2015-31) Under the *wakadoshiyori* were the *[metsuke](/wiki/Metsuke "Metsuke")*.

Some *shōguns* appointed a *soba yōnin*. This person acted as a liaison between the *shōgun* and the *rōjū*. The *soba yōnin* increased in importance during the time of the fifth *shōgun* [Tokugawa Tsunayoshi](/wiki/Tokugawa_Tsunayoshi "Tokugawa Tsunayoshi"), when a wakadoshiyori, [Inaba Masayasu](/wiki/Inaba_Masayasu "Inaba Masayasu"), assassinated [Hotta Masatoshi](/wiki/Hotta_Masatoshi "Hotta Masatoshi"), the *tairō*. Fearing for his personal safety, Tsunayoshi moved the *rōjū* to a more distant part of the castle. Some of the most famous *soba yōnin* were [Yanagisawa Yoshiyasu](/wiki/Yanagisawa_Yoshiyasu "Yanagisawa Yoshiyasu") and [Tanuma Okitsugu](/wiki/Tanuma_Okitsugu "Tanuma Okitsugu").

### Ōmetsuke and metsuke

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tokugawa_shogunate&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Ōmetsuke and metsuke")]

The *ōmetsuke* and *[metsuke](/wiki/Metsuke "Metsuke")* were officials who reported to the *rōjū* and *wakadoshiyori*.[[29]](#cite_note-mikiso_perez_2015-31) The five *ōmetsuke* were in charge of monitoring the affairs of the *daimyō*, *kuge* and imperial court. They were in charge of discovering any threat of rebellion. Early in the Edo period, *daimyō* such as [Yagyū Munefuyu](/wiki/Yagy%C5%AB_Munefuyu "Yagyū Munefuyu") held the office. Soon, however, it fell to *[hatamoto](/wiki/Hatamoto "Hatamoto")* with rankings of 5,000 *koku* or more. To give them authority in their dealings with *daimyō*, they were often ranked at 10,000 *koku* and given the title of *[kami](/wiki/Kokushi_(official) "Kokushi (official)")* (an ancient title, typically signifying the governor of a [province](/wiki/Provinces_of_Japan "Provinces of Japan")) such as *Bizen-no-kami*.

As time progressed, the function of the *ōmetsuke* evolved into one of passing orders from the shogunate to the *daimyō*, and of administering to ceremonies within Edo Castle. They also took on additional responsibilities such as supervising religious affairs and controlling firearms. The *metsuke*, reporting to the *wakadoshiyori*, oversaw the affairs of the vassals of the *shōgun*.[[29]](#cite_note-mikiso_perez_2015-31) They were the police force for the thousands of hatamoto and [gokenin](/wiki/Gokenin "Gokenin") who were concentrated in Edo. Individual *han* had their own *metsuke* who similarly policed their samurai.

### San-bugyō

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tokugawa_shogunate&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: San-bugyō")]

The *san-[bugyō](/wiki/Bugy%C5%8D "Bugyō")* (三奉行 "three administrators") were the *jisha*, *kanjō*, and *[machi-bugyō](/wiki/Machi-bugy%C5%8D "Machi-bugyō")*, which respectively oversaw [temples](/wiki/Buddhist_temples_in_Japan "Buddhist temples in Japan") and [shrines](/wiki/Shinto_shrine "Shinto shrine"), accounting, and the cities. The *[jisha-bugyō](/wiki/Jisha-bugy%C5%8D "Jisha-bugyō")* had the highest status of the three. They oversaw the administration of Buddhist temples (*ji*) and Shinto shrines (*sha*), many of which held fiefs. Also, they heard lawsuits from several land holdings outside the eight [Kantō](/wiki/Kant%C5%8D_region "Kantō region") provinces. The appointments normally went to *daimyō*; [Ōoka Tadasuke](/wiki/%C5%8Coka_Tadasuke "Ōoka Tadasuke") was an exception, though he later became a *daimyō*.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The *[kanjō-bugyō](/wiki/Kanj%C5%8D-bugy%C5%8D "Kanjō-bugyō")* were next in status. The four holders of this office reported to the *rōjū*. They were responsible for the finances of the shogunate.[[59]](#cite_note-61)

The *machi-bugyō* were the chief city administrators of Edo and other cities. Their roles included mayor, chief of the police (and, later, also of the fire department), and judge in criminal and civil matters not involving samurai. Two (briefly, three) men, normally hatamoto, held the office, and alternated by month.[[60]](#cite_note-62)

### Tenryō, gundai and daikan

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tokugawa_shogunate&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Tenryō, gundai and daikan")]

The *san-bugyō* together sat on a council called the *[hyōjōsho](/wiki/Hy%C5%8Dj%C5%8Dsho "Hyōjōsho")*. In this capacity, they were responsible for administering the *tenryō* (the shogun's estates), supervising the *gundai* ([郡代](https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E9%83%A1%E4%BB%A3 "ja:郡代")), the *[daikan](/wiki/Daikan "Daikan")* and the *kura bugyō* ([蔵奉行](https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E8%94%B5%E5%A5%89%E8%A1%8C "ja:蔵奉行")), as well as hearing cases involving samurai. The *gundai* managed Tokugawa domains with incomes greater than 10,000 koku while the *daikan* managed areas with incomes between 5,000 and 10,000 koku.

The shogun directly held lands in various parts of Japan. These were known as *shihaisho* (支配所); since the Meiji period, the term *tenryō* ([天領](https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E5%A4%A9%E9%A0%98 "ja:天領"), literally "Emperor's land") has become synonymous, because the shogun's lands were returned to the emperor.[[61]](#cite_note-63) In addition to the territory that Ieyasu held prior to the Battle of Sekigahara, this included lands he gained in that battle and lands gained as a result of the [Summer and Winter Sieges of Osaka](/wiki/Siege_of_Osaka "Siege of Osaka"). Major cities as Nagasaki and Osaka, and [mines](/wiki/Mining "Mining"), including the [Sado](/wiki/Sado_Province "Sado Province") [gold mine](/wiki/Gold_mine "Gold mine"), also fell into this category.

### Gaikoku bugyō

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tokugawa_shogunate&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Gaikoku bugyō")]

The *[gaikoku bugyō](/wiki/Gaikoku_bugy%C5%8D "Gaikoku bugyō")* were administrators appointed between 1858 and 1868. They were charged with overseeing trade and diplomatic relations with foreign countries, and were based in the [treaty ports](/wiki/Treaty_ports "Treaty ports") of Nagasaki and Kanagawa (Yokohama).[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]
